name: volumes
context: Persistent storage with Docker volumes — named volumes, bind mounts, tmpfs
info: Docker containers are ephemeral — data written to the container's writable layer is lost when the container is removed. Volumes provide persistent storage that survives container lifecycle events. Named volumes are managed by Docker and stored in `/var/lib/docker/volumes/`. Bind mounts map a host directory into the container — ideal for development with live code reload. tmpfs mounts store data in memory only, useful for secrets and temporary data. This guide covers volume types, creation and management, backup and restore strategies, and integration with Docker Compose.
body:
# Docker Volumes

Containers are ephemeral — when a container is removed, its writable layer is lost. Volumes provide persistent storage that survives container lifecycle events (stop, restart, remove). Docker offers three mount types for different use cases.

## Volume Types

| Mount Type | Managed By | Persistence | Use Case |
|------------|-----------|-------------|----------|
| Named volume | Docker | Survives container removal | Database data, application state |
| Bind mount | Host filesystem | Host directory | Development (live reload), config files |
| tmpfs | Memory | Lost on container stop | Secrets, temporary data, caches |

### Named Volumes

Named volumes are the preferred mechanism for persistent data. Docker manages the storage location (typically `/var/lib/docker/volumes/`).

```bash
# Create a named volume
docker volume create pgdata

# Run a container with the volume
docker run -d \
  --name postgres \
  -v pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data \
  postgres:16-alpine

# Volume survives container removal
docker rm -f postgres
docker volume ls  # pgdata still exists

# Start a new container with the same volume — data intact
docker run -d \
  --name postgres-new \
  -v pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data \
  postgres:16-alpine
```

In [[docker/compose]], declare named volumes in the top-level `volumes:` section:

```yaml
services:
  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data

volumes:
  pgdata:
```

See [[docker/compose#volumes-networks]] for details.

### Bind Mounts

Bind mounts map a host directory directly into the container. Changes on either side are immediately visible to the other.

```bash
# Mount current directory into the container
docker run -d \
  --name dev \
  -v $(pwd):/app \
  -w /app \
  node:20-alpine \
  npm run dev

# Read-only bind mount for config
docker run -d \
  -v /host/config/nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf:ro \
  nginx:alpine
```

Bind mounts are essential for development workflows — edit code on the host and see changes immediately in the container. Pair with file watchers for live reload. See [[docker/images/build#best-practices]] for how this complements the build workflow.

**Caution:** Bind mounts depend on the host filesystem structure. They are less portable than named volumes. Use named volumes for production data.

### tmpfs Mounts

tmpfs mounts store data in the host's memory. Data is never written to disk and is lost when the container stops.

```bash
# tmpfs mount for temporary data
docker run -d \
  --tmpfs /app/tmp:size=100m \
  myapp:latest

# Using --mount syntax (more explicit)
docker run -d \
  --mount type=tmpfs,destination=/app/tmp,tmpfs-size=104857600 \
  myapp:latest
```

Use tmpfs for:
- Secrets that should never touch disk
- Build caches and temporary files
- Scratch space for data processing

## Backup and Restore

Back up named volumes by mounting them into a temporary container:

```bash
# Backup volume to a tar archive
docker run --rm \
  -v pgdata:/source:ro \
  -v $(pwd):/backup \
  alpine \
  tar czf /backup/pgdata-backup.tar.gz -C /source .

# Restore from backup
docker run --rm \
  -v pgdata:/target \
  -v $(pwd):/backup:ro \
  alpine \
  tar xzf /backup/pgdata-backup.tar.gz -C /target
```

For production databases, prefer the database's native dump/restore tools (e.g., `pg_dump`, `mysqldump`) over filesystem-level backups.

## Volume Management

```bash
docker volume ls                    # list all volumes
docker volume inspect pgdata        # show volume details
docker volume rm pgdata             # remove a volume
docker volume prune                 # remove all unused volumes
```

**Warning:** `docker volume prune` removes all volumes not currently mounted by a container. Always verify before pruning in production.

## Sharing Volumes Between Containers

Multiple containers can mount the same named volume for shared data:

```bash
# Writer container
docker run -d --name writer -v shared-data:/data alpine sh -c 'while true; do date >> /data/log.txt; sleep 5; done'

# Reader container
docker run -d --name reader -v shared-data:/data:ro alpine tail -f /data/log.txt
```

In [[docker/compose]], multiple services can reference the same named volume. Use `:ro` for read-only access where appropriate.

sections.title: Volume types
sections.body: Named volumes are managed by Docker and persist independently of containers — use for databases and application state. Bind mounts map host directories into containers — use for development with live code reload. tmpfs mounts store data in memory only — use for secrets and temporary data. Named volumes are the most portable and recommended for production.

sections.title: Named volumes
sections.body: Create with `docker volume create` or declare in [[docker/compose]] under the `volumes:` top-level key. Mount with `-v name:/path` or `--mount type=volume,source=name,target=/path`. Data survives container removal. Stored in `/var/lib/docker/volumes/` on Linux. Multiple containers can share a named volume. Use for databases (see [[docker/compose#services]]), caches, and any data that must persist.

sections.title: Bind mounts
sections.body: Map host paths directly into containers with `-v /host/path:/container/path`. Changes are bidirectional and immediate. Essential for development workflows — edit on host, see changes in container. Use `:ro` for read-only mounts (config files, certificates). Less portable than named volumes — depend on host filesystem. See [[docker/images/build]] for how bind mounts complement image builds during development.

sections.title: Backup and restore
sections.body: Back up named volumes by mounting into a temporary `alpine` container and creating a tar archive. Restore by extracting the archive into the volume. For databases, prefer native dump tools (`pg_dump`, `mysqldump`) over filesystem backups. Automate backups with cron jobs or scheduled containers. Test restores regularly to verify backup integrity.

links.context: Build workflow paired with bind mounts for development
Networking paired with volumes for data services

related.note: Compose is the standard way to declare and manage volumes
Networking and volumes together define how services communicate and persist data
Bind mounts during development complement the build workflow
